paper_metadata.title: How to win public support for financial climate policies: both climate anxiety and economic anticipation work
paper_metadata.authors: Yang Li, Yuxuan Liang, Jianchi Tian, Yan Sun, Meijun Meng
paper_metadata.empirical_scope_or_sample: Nationwide longitudinal survey; 2322 valid respondents in China
paper_metadata.carbon_tax_relevance_summary: Directly analyzes public support for carbon tax in China and estimates determinants of CT support using longitudinal survey data.

determinants.definition: Worry or concern about climate change measured at T1.
determinants.mechanism: Increases attention to climate harms and perceived climate benefits, which can raise support, but the direct T1 effect on CT support is not significant.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Support for carbon tax (CT) in a national longitudinal survey of Chinese respondents.
determinants.evidence_quote: Climate anxiety prominently influenced CT support through perceived climate benefit.
determinants.page_or_section: 3.1 Results for CT support; Appendix A
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Indirect effect 0.056, p<0.001; direct β=0.019, p>0.1.

determinants.definition: Worry or concern about climate change measured at T2.
determinants.mechanism: Higher contemporaneous climate anxiety increases perceived climate benefits and directly raises CT support.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Support for carbon tax (CT) in a national longitudinal survey of Chinese respondents.
determinants.evidence_quote: The influence of climate anxiety at T2 could directly affect CT support at T2.
determinants.page_or_section: 3.1 Results for CT support; Appendix A
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Direct β=0.093, p<0.001; indirect effect 0.122, p<0.001.

determinants.definition: Expectation about local economic growth or future economic conditions measured at T1.
determinants.mechanism: Positive economic outlook increases support mainly by raising perceived personal benefits, though the direct T1 effect on CT support is not significant.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Support for carbon tax (CT) in a national longitudinal survey of Chinese respondents.
determinants.evidence_quote: Economic anticipation at T1 could not directly affect CT support at T2 either.
determinants.page_or_section: 3.1 Results for CT support; Appendix A
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Indirect effect 0.008, p=0.001; direct β=0.027, p>0.1.

determinants.definition: Expectation about local economic growth or future economic conditions measured at T2.
determinants.mechanism: Greater optimism about the economy increases CT support directly and through higher perceived personal benefits.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Support for carbon tax (CT) in a national longitudinal survey of Chinese respondents.
determinants.evidence_quote: Economic anticipation at T2 directly worked on CT support.
determinants.page_or_section: 3.1 Results for CT support; Appendix A
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Direct β=0.09, p<0.001; indirect effect 0.016, p<0.001.

determinants.definition: Belief that the carbon tax effectively mitigates climate change.
determinants.mechanism: Perceived climate effectiveness increases willingness to support the tax.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Support for carbon tax (CT) in a national longitudinal survey of Chinese respondents.
determinants.evidence_quote: Perceived climate benefit (β = 0.522, p < 0.001) worked better than perceived personal benefit on CT support.
determinants.page_or_section: 3.1 Results for CT support; Appendix A

determinants.definition: Belief that the carbon tax brings personal economic or practical benefits.
determinants.mechanism: Perceived private gains make support for the tax stronger.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Support for carbon tax (CT) in a national longitudinal survey of Chinese respondents.
determinants.page_or_section: 3.1 Results for CT support; Appendix A

determinants.mechanism: Older respondents were more supportive of CT; no specific mechanism given in the paper.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Support for carbon tax (CT) in a national longitudinal survey of Chinese respondents.
determinants.notes: Demographic control significant in CT model.

exclusions_or_uncertainties.issue: Non-significant demographic controls in CT model
exclusions_or_uncertainties.reason: Gender, income, residence place, and education were reported but not statistically significant for CT support; omitted to keep only directly supported determinants.
Climate anxiety and economic anticipation were measured at T1 and T2; T1 paths were indirect or nonsignificant, while T2 paths were directly positive.
